title: Infrastructure as Code (IaC)
description: Infrastructure as Code concepts for SAP engineers transitioning to FAANG. Covers Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi, immutable infrastructure vs mutable infrastructure, configuration management (Ansible, Chef), and how these replace manual SAP landscape configuration, instance provisioning, and transport-based change management.

sections.title: What Is Infrastructure as Code? (SAP Context)

description.value:
In SAP, infrastructure is configured manually: the Basis team installs systems via SWPM or SAPinst, applies SAP Notes through SNOTE, configures instance profiles, sets up RFC destinations in SM59, manages printer configuration via SPAD, and maintains system copies with scripts. Every environment is slightly different because manual steps are skipped, commands are run out of order, or configuration parameters drift over time. This creates the classic snowflake server problem: each SAP system is unique, unreproducible, and terrifying to replace.

In FAANG, infrastructure is defined entirely as code. Every server, load balancer, database, network rule, and DNS record is described in version-controlled configuration files. These files go through code review (just like ABAP code review in Code Inspector or ATC), are tested in CI/CD pipelines, and deployed automatically. The result: infrastructure is reproducible (anyone can recreate the exact same environment), auditable (every change has a commit history), and recoverable (a terminated production cluster can be rebuilt from scratch in minutes).

The mental model shift is profound. An SAP Basis engineer thinks of infrastructure as a set of physical or virtual machines that need ongoing care and feeding. A FAANG infrastructure engineer thinks of infrastructure as a snapshot of configuration files that can be destroyed and recreated at will. This is the core difference between pets (servers you name and care for) and cattle (servers that are numbered, interchangeable, and disposable). SAP landscapes are typically managed as pets. FAANG treats everything as cattle.

Terraform by HashiCorp is the most widely adopted IaC tool in the industry. It uses a declarative language called HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) to define cloud resources: compute instances, virtual networks, load balancers, DNS records, IAM policies, and more. The fundamental workflow is: init (download providers and modules), plan (show a diff between the current state and desired state — this is Terraform's killer feature), apply (execute the changes), and destroy (tear everything down). State management is handled through a state file (local or remote in S3, Terraform Cloud, or Azure Storage), which maps configuration to real-world resources.

Compare this to SAP provisioning. When you create a new SAP system, the Basis team follows an installation guide: (1) request VMs from the virtualization team, (2) install the operating system, (3) run SAPinst with a parameter file, (4) apply kernel patches, (5) configure the instance profile, (6) set up the database, (7) restore a system copy or configure initial data. This process takes days to weeks, is error-prone, and produces a unique system that cannot be exactly reproduced. With Terraform, a system can be provisioned in minutes by running a single apply command, and the entire infrastructure definition is a set of .tf files in a Git repository.

Pulumi extends the IaC concept further by allowing you to define infrastructure using general-purpose programming languages (TypeScript, Python, Go, C#, Java) instead of a domain-specific language like HCL. This means you can use loops, functions, conditionals, and your existing IDE features (autocomplete, refactoring, testing) to manage infrastructure. Pulumi also supports state management, diff-based previews, and multi-cloud deployment. For SAP engineers who already know JavaScript, Python, or Java, Pulumi offers a shallower learning curve than HCL. AWS CloudFormation is Amazon's native IaC service, using JSON or YAML templates. It is deeply integrated with AWS (IAM roles, stack policies, change sets) but is AWS-only, unlike Terraform and Pulumi which are multi-cloud.

sections.title: Immutable Infrastructure — Why FAANG Replaces, Never Repairs

description.value:
Mutable infrastructure is the traditional approach: when a server needs an update, you SSH into it (or log in via SAP GUI or SAP HANA Studio), apply patches, modify configuration files, restart services, and the server continues running. Over time, each server accumulates unique modifications. Configuration drift sets in: server A has package version 1.2, server B has version 1.3 because the patch was applied on a different date. When a critical issue arises, no one knows the exact state of any given server. This is the default mode for SAP landscapes: every system (DEV, QAS, PRD) has accumulated years of manual changes, SAP Notes, transport requests, and custom configurations that make them all slightly different.

Immutable infrastructure is the FAANG approach: never modify a running server. Instead, build a new golden image (AMI, container image, VM template) with all changes baked in, deploy it to production, route traffic to the new servers, and terminate the old ones. If you need to install a security patch, you do not patch the running servers — you build a new image with the patch, deploy it, and destroy the old ones. This eliminates configuration drift entirely because every server in the fleet is an exact replica built from the same image. Rolling back is trivial: just redeploy the previous image.

The SAP equivalent of immutable infrastructure is a system refresh: you rebuild a Quality Assurance system from a Production backup. In the SAP world, this is a painful, multi-day manual process involving system copy, post-processing activities (SAP Note application, transport import, user sync), and regression testing. In the immutable world, a system refresh is: delete the old servers, deploy a new image built from the production golden image, and route traffic. Total time: minutes. The Staff+ lesson: when you hear an interviewer ask about immutable infrastructure, frame it in terms of SAP's system refresh process and explain how IaC turns a painful multi-day operation into a trivial automated command.

Configuration management tools (Ansible, Chef, Puppet, SaltStack) automate the installation, configuration, and ongoing management of software on servers. Where Terraform provisions the infrastructure (servers, networks, load balancers), configuration management tools handle what runs on those servers: packages, services, configuration files, application deployments.

Ansible is the most popular choice for SAP-adjacent workloads because it is agentless (uses SSH, no software to install on target servers), uses YAML for playbooks (human-readable), and has a large collection of community modules including SAP-specific modules from Red Hat and the community. An Ansible playbook looks like: install SAP Host Agent, copy instance profile, configure kernel parameters, start SAP system. These playbooks are idempotent: running them multiple times produces the same result, and they only make changes when the actual state differs from the desired state.

Chef uses a Ruby DSL (domain-specific language) to define resources and their desired state. Chef follows a pull model: a Chef client runs on each server, polls a Chef server for the latest policy, and converges the server to match the policy. Chef is more complex than Ansible but offers better scalability for very large fleets (10,000+ servers). Puppet is similar to Chef but uses its own declarative language and uses a master-agent architecture. SaltStack (Salt) combines a YAML DSL with a fast, event-driven communication layer using ZeroMQ.

The SAP equivalent: applying SAP Notes via SNOTE is a manual configuration management task. The SAP Solution Manager / Focused Run can automate some note application, but the process is still primarily manual. An Ansible playbook that applies a set of SAP Notes, adjusts kernel parameters, updates the instance profile, and restarts the system is what a FAANG engineer would write instead of maintaining a spreadsheet of which notes have been applied to which systems. The key difference: SAP's approach is event-driven (a note is released, you apply it), while Ansible's approach is state-driven (the system is always in the desired state, regardless of how it got there).

The SAP Transport Management System (TMS) is the closest concept in the SAP world to IaC. Both manage the lifecycle of changes through environment promotion (Dev → Test → Prod), both require approval gates, and both maintain an audit trail. However, the similarities end at the abstraction level, speed, and automation capability.

SAP Transports manage ABAP code changes (programs, function modules, classes, dictionary objects) through the SAP system landscape. A transport request is created in the development system, released to the consolidation layer, imported into QA via STMS_IMPORT, and finally imported into production via a change management process (ChaRM or external tool like SolMan). The transport moves ABAP code between systems via RFC-based communication. The transport itself is a file (cofiles + data files) stored on the transport directory share. This is elegant for ABAP code but limited: it only handles ABAP development objects, not infrastructure, not configuration, not non-ABAP components.

IaC changes manage any cloud resource: compute, network, storage, DNS, IAM, monitoring, logging, CI/CD pipelines. The change is a configuration file (HCL, YAML, JSON) that goes through Git-based code review, automated testing, and CI/CD deployment. The scope is infinitely broader than SAP transports. A single IaC change can provision a 200-node Kubernetes cluster, configure a global CDN, and set up monitoring dashboards. An SAP transport can import a Z-report to a single ABAP system.

details: Staff+ Insight: IaC is the closest FAANG concept to SAP's transport system — both are about managing changes to environments through code. The difference: SAP transports are specialized (only for ABAP code), slow (days to promote), and manual (transport organizer clicks buttons). IaC changes are generic (any cloud resource), fast (minutes to deploy), and automated (CI/CD pipeline). As an SAP engineer, you already understand the 'promote changes through environments' mental model. Apply it to infrastructure, and IaC will feel natural. Start by writing a Terraform script that provisions a single cloud resource (like an AWS S3 bucket) and see how it compares to creating an SAP transport request.
